dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a sick person at --Address Redacted-- in the city of [CITY].
Hi ma'am, I'm Alex and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm feeling very weak and dizzy. It happened a few minutes ago while sitting outside.
I see you're awake. Let me assess how alert you are now.
I need you to tell me your full name and the day of the week.
Can you raise your right arm for me? How about both arms together?
Your airway is clear and there's no sign of obstruction or unusual posture.
You're breathing regularly. Does it feel comfortable for you?
I'd like to take your pulse and check your blood pressure now.
Let me take your blood pressure. It's 72 over 43.
Your pulse is 58, with a regular rhythm.
Your breathing rate is 16 per minute and seems unlabored.
I'll now put a clip on your finger to measure your oxygen saturation.
I need to check your blood sugar. It is currently 136.
Are you experiencing any abdominal pain or discomfort?
Do you have any known allergies to medications or food?
Are you currently taking any medications for chronic or recent conditions?
Have you ever had conditions like heart disease or diabetes?
Have you been eating and drinking regularly over the past couple of days?
Did anything specific happen before you started feeling weak and dizzy?
Your level of responsiveness is normal. Your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15, I'll note that.
I don't see any rashes, bruises, scars, or signs of a bite on your skin.
Let me examine your neck gently for any lymph nodes or tenderness.
I'll now check your mouth and throat for signs of obstruction or discomfort.
I'm going to listen to your heart and lungs with a stethoscope.
We are starting an IV and giving fluids to help improve your blood pressure.
I'm placing these pads to check your heart rhythm.
The heart rhythm is a normal sinus rhythm. No concerning findings at this time.
Let me retake your blood pressure now. It improved to 87 over 57.
Your oxygen saturation dropped slightly to 88, but your other vitals are stable.
The last blood pressure measurement is 92 over 56. Your readings are trending up.
We're following the Medical - Hypotension/Shock (Nontrauma) protocol per standing orders.
You've requested transport to [COMPANY_NAME]. That remains the destination.
Let's move you to the stretcher. Can you stand as best as you can?
You're now secured on the stretcher with straps and the rails have been lowered.
I'm going to gently feel your abdomen for any tenderness or masses.
I've had constipation and abdominal pain for several days.
Have you had any blood in your stools or changes in urination?
Our earlier reading showed a blood sugar of 136. That was around the time of your initial symptoms.
Your vitals are trending upward. You're showing some improvement.
The transport to [COMPANY_NAME] is starting now.
How do you feel right now? Any new symptoms or concerns?
The fluids are continuing and you're not receiving any additional medications at this time.
The ECG results are normal for a female in her 80s, nothing concerning found.
I'll continue to monitor your vitals, especially blood pressure and heart rate.
You're secured on the stretcher, and the rails are in place for transport.
For documentation, can you share your full name and age?
Your constipation could be contributing to the low blood pressure you're experiencing.
The ride to the hospital will take about fifteen minutes. Let me know if you need anything.
I will continue routine check-ups on your blood pressure and oxygen levels.
Your most recent readings are 92 over 56 blood pressure and heart rate of 58.
Let me check your extremities for signs of reduced sensation or injury.
Peripheral pulses are weak but present. No deformities noted.
I'll now look at your back for any signs of pain or discomfort. Press any areas?
You appear more stable, so the move to the hospital should be smooth.
The hospital is aware of the fluids we've started and the improvement in your vitals.
Your low blood pressure seems likely due to a hypovolemic issue, possibly from constipation.
Your main issues are weakness, dizziness, and discomfort from prolonged constipation.
We're still en route and will keep monitoring your condition. Let us know if needed.
The hospital staff have been notified and are prepared to receive you on arrival.
The last oxygen saturation reading is 99% - remaining in the normal range.
We're arriving at [COMPANY_NAME] now and will transfer you to the emergency staff.
The nurse will take over. I've provided a report of our care so far.
I've given a complete care report to emergency staff and remain with you.
All documentation about your care has been completed and shared with the hospital team.
Vital readings have remained stable throughout the care and transport.
The care has been fully handed over to the hospital medical team.
The hospital team now has access to your full medical record.
If not already scheduled, make sure to follow up with your primary care physician.
The hospital will assess your condition and determine what additional care you need.
Please speak to the hospital staff about arranging follow-up care once discharged.
Your care team is aware of the possible link between constipation and your symptoms.
We'll remain with you until the hospital takes over your care.
Emergency care is fully transitioned to the hospital staff now.
